questions.question: A buyer has marginal reservation prices for slices of pizza of $8, $6, $4, and $1. The market price is $5 per slice, and the buyer can buy only whole slices. How many slices maximize net surplus, and what does this imply for the individual demand schedule?

choices.A: 1 slice, because the first slice has the highest reservation price.
choices.B: 2 slices, because the first two marginal values exceed price while the third does not.
choices.C: 3 slices, because total value $18 exceeds total cost $15.
choices.D: 4 slices, because positive marginal value means positive demand.

questions.explanation: The buyer purchases units whose marginal reservation price is at least the $5 price: $8 and $6 only. A stops too soon, C includes a negative marginal-surplus third unit, and D ignores price.

questions.question: A seller can supply up to four handmade mugs with marginal costs $3, $5, $9, and $14. The market price is $10 per mug. What quantity maximizes producer surplus, and how does this generate the supply schedule?

choices.A: 2 mugs, because only costs strictly below average price should be produced.
choices.B: 3 mugs, because the first three marginal costs are no greater than price.
choices.C: 4 mugs, because total revenue $40 exceeds total cost $31.
choices.D: 1 mug, because the lowest-cost unit determines supply.

questions.explanation: Produce units with marginal cost at or below price: 3, 5, and 9. C includes the fourth unit with marginal cost 14 > price, lowering surplus. A and D stop too early.

questions.question: An individual demand schedule for coffee is Qd = 12 - 2P. If the price rises from $3 to $4, what happens to quantity demanded, and what mechanism explains the movement?

choices.A: Quantity demanded falls from 6 to 4 because the higher price moves the buyer up along the same demand schedule.
choices.B: Quantity demanded rises from 6 to 8 because higher price signals higher quality.
choices.C: Demand shifts left by 2 because price is an exogenous shifter.
choices.D: Quantity demanded falls from 9 to 8 because the price rose by 1.

questions.explanation: At P = 3, Qd = 6; at P = 4, Qd = 4. This is movement along demand due to price. B invents quality signaling, C confuses movement with shift, and D uses the wrong calculation.

questions.question: A supply schedule is Qs = -6 + 3P. A technology improvement changes it to Qs = -2 + 3P. At P = $5, what happens to quantity supplied, and why?

choices.A: It falls from 9 to 5 because better technology lowers price.
choices.B: It rises from 9 to 13 because lower marginal cost shifts supply outward at each price.
choices.C: It rises from 15 to 17 because price is multiplied by technology.
choices.D: It is unchanged because price did not change.

questions.explanation: Old Qs = -6 + 15 = 9; new Qs = -2 + 15 = 13. Better technology shifts supply outward. A answers an equilibrium price question, C miscalculates, and D ignores supply shifters.

questions.question: Market demand is created by horizontally summing two individual demands: Q1 = 20 - P and Q2 = 10 - 0.5P for prices where both quantities are positive. What is the market demand slope in Q-on-P form over that range?

choices.A: -1.5, because the quantities demanded are added at each price.
choices.B: -0.5, because only the flatter individual curve matters.
choices.C: -1, because individual 1 has the larger intercept.
choices.D: 1.5, because market quantity rises when price rises.

questions.explanation: Horizontal summation gives Q = 30 - 1.5P, so the slope in Q-on-P form is -1.5. B and C ignore one consumer, and D gets the sign wrong.

questions.question: Three buyers have reservation prices $12, $9, and $5 for one unit each. What does the market demand schedule look like verbally?

choices.A: A vertical line at Q = 3 for all positive prices.
choices.B: An upward-sloping step curve with steps at $5, $9, and $12.
choices.C: A downward step curve: Q = 3 for P <= $5, Q = 2 for $5 < P <= $9, Q = 1 for $9 < P <= $12, and Q = 0 for P > $12.
choices.D: A smooth line with slope -3 because there are three buyers.

questions.explanation: As price rises, buyers with lower reservation prices exit, creating a downward step schedule. A ignores price thresholds, B has the slope direction wrong, and D incorrectly smooths discrete buyers.

questions.question: A demand schedule says Qd = 40 at P = $6. What does this statement mean economically?

choices.A: Exactly 40 units will be sold regardless of supply.
choices.B: Buyers are willing to purchase 40 units at a price of $6, holding other demand shifters fixed.
choices.C: Sellers must produce 40 units at a price of $6.
choices.D: Every buyer values the good at exactly $6.

questions.explanation: A demand schedule gives quantity buyers want at each price, ceteris paribus. A assumes market clearing, C describes supply, and D confuses market quantity with individual valuations.

questions.question: A campus store records how many hoodies students would buy at prices $20, $30, and $40, then adds those quantities across students. Which concept is being constructed?

choices.A: A market demand schedule, because quantities demanded by individuals are summed at each price.
choices.B: A production function, because hoodies are outputs.
choices.C: A budget line, because prices appear in the data.
choices.D: A supply schedule, because the store sells hoodies.

questions.explanation: Adding individual quantities at each price constructs market demand. B concerns input-output production, C concerns affordability for one consumer, and D would require seller quantities supplied.

questions.question: Which statement best distinguishes a demand schedule from quantity demanded?

choices.A: A demand schedule is the whole relationship between price and quantity demanded; quantity demanded is the amount buyers want at one particular price.
choices.B: A demand schedule is one point; quantity demanded is the whole curve.
choices.C: A demand schedule applies to sellers; quantity demanded applies to buyers.
choices.D: A demand schedule includes only income; quantity demanded includes only price.

questions.explanation: Demand is the relationship; quantity demanded is a point on that relationship. B reverses them, C confuses demand and supply, and D misstates both concepts.

questions.question: Market demand is P = 20 - Q and market supply is P = 4 + Q. At the competitive equilibrium, what is total surplus?

choices.A: 64, because equilibrium Q = 8 and total surplus is the triangle between demand and supply.
choices.B: 128, because total surplus equals P times Q.
choices.D: 80, because the demand intercept times quantity is 20 x 4.

questions.explanation: Equilibrium solves 20 - Q = 4 + Q, so Q = 8 and P = 12. Total surplus is 0.5(20 - 4)(8) = 64. B uses revenue, C counts only part of surplus, and D uses the wrong base and height.
